planting.note: Divide established clumps after flowering or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade; nodding clivia has a more pendulous flower than C. miniata and produces longer, narrower leaves. Like all clivias, it requires a dry winter rest period to trigger reliable spring flowering — reduce watering significantly from late autumn through winter. All parts are toxic if ingested.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; nodding clivia is the first described species in the Clivia genus and is the most pendulous-flowered — producing in spring a drooping umbel of 20–40 tubular-funnel flowers that hang downward more decisively than C. miniata, in shades of orange-red to salmon with yellow-green tips on the outer petal margins, creating a pendant, chandelier-like cluster of nodding tubes. The leaf blades are narrower and longer than C. miniata, with a slightly wavy or recurved texture. It grows naturally in the dense, humid, coastal forests of the Eastern Cape near the sea, an environment very different from the drier, higher-altitude KwaZulu-Natal forests of C. miniata. In cultivation it provides the same outstanding shade tolerance — thriving in the deep, dry shade under large trees — with the somewhat less spectacular but more refined pendant flower form. Like C. miniata, it is evergreen, long-lived, and requires the dry winter rest period to flower. A plant for clivia collectors and those who appreciate the more nuanced, quiet elegance of the drooping flower form over the bold upward-facing clusters of C. miniata.

common_name: Arabian Star of Bethlehem (Black-Centered Star Flower)

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in fall in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Arabian star of Bethlehem grows in late winter through spring, blooms in late spring, then goes summer dormant. In zones 7b–10 it can remain in the ground through summer dormancy with withheld water; lift and store dry in colder zones. An excellent cut flower with long vase life.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in well-drained, lean soil in full sun; Arabian star of Bethlehem is one of the most distinctive and fragrant spring-blooming bulbs — a Mediterranean and North African native producing in late spring (May–June), tall, upright scapes bearing a raceme of large (2 inch), pure-white, six-petaled, cup-shaped flowers each with a dramatic, prominent, dark, blue-black to jet-black ovary at the center — a shining black jewel at the heart of each snow-white flower creating a startling, bold contrast of white and black that gives the blooms an immediately recognizable and distinctive appearance unlike any other common spring bulb. The fragrance is sweet and noticeable, particularly in the evening. The flowers are excellent cut flowers with exceptional vase life of 2–3 weeks, making this a valuable bulb for cutting gardens. The large, broad, strap-shaped leaves are present from autumn through late spring, then die back in summer dormancy. Outstanding in zones 7b–10 where it can remain in the ground as a perennial; in zone 9a Gulf Coast gardens it naturalizes effectively. A superb, slightly unusual spring-flowering bulb for the cutting garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs just below the soil surface in very well-drained, sandy, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in fall; frizzle sizzle follows the Cape winter-rainfall growth cycle — growing and flowering in winter and spring, then going summer dormant. Keep dry in summer dormancy. Full sun maximizes the dramatic spiral leaf curling — shaded plants may produce less tightly curled foliage.

directions: Plant bulbs near the soil surface in very well-drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun; frizzle sizzle is a South African Cape bulb that has attracted unusual attention from collectors and gardeners primarily for its extraordinary foliage — the leaves are not flat strap-like leaves but tightly spiral, corkscrew-curled, like a pig's tail or a phone cord, creating an immediately bizarre and eye-catching effect quite unlike any other commonly grown plant. The leaves, each spiraling through one to several complete rotations as they grow, create a tightly curled, wispy, almost spring-like cluster of spirals at ground level that is so unusual it reliably draws a second look from every observer. In late winter through spring it produces small, nodding, greenish-yellow to yellow, vanilla-fragrant flowers on short stems — pleasant but secondary to the extraordinary foliage. The spiral curling is a genuine botanical trait of the species rather than a cultivated mutation. It grows naturally in the sandy coastal heathlands of the Western Cape. Outstanding as a container plant where the unique spiral foliage can be examined closely and protected from summer rain during dormancy.

common_name: African False Lily of the Valley (Little White Soldiers)

planting.note: Divide offset bulblets or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade to part shade in spring; African false lily of the valley thrives in conditions of consistent shade and moisture that simulate its forest-floor native habitat. It goes semi-dormant in winter with reduced watering but is never fully deciduous in mild climates. Excellent for deep shade where bulbs that need more light would fail.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full to part shade; African false lily of the valley is a South African forest-floor bulb prized for two qualities — its shade tolerance (making it one of the few true bulbs that thrives in deep shade) and the attractive dark-spotted pattern of its leaves (the species epithet maculata means 'spotted'). The broadly oval, smooth, dark-green leaves are irregularly decorated with darker green to bronze-purple spots and blotches that give each leaf a painted, dappled quality reminiscent of certain Pulmonaria or Arum species. In late spring through summer it produces dense, upright racemes of small, white, six-petaled, star-shaped flowers similar in form to lily of the valley but upright rather than pendant. The small, crowded, bright-white flowers create a cheerful, clean display. It spreads by producing numerous small offset bulblets, quickly forming a tight, spreading colony in favorable conditions. An outstanding choice for zones 9–11 where a shade-tolerant, spotted-leaved, white-flowering bulb groundcover is needed under dense tree canopy.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in fall (or spring) in lean, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; spiny chicory grows naturally on sea cliffs and rocky coastal slopes exposed to wind and salt spray. It is notably salt-tolerant and wind-resistant. Harvest young shoots and leaves for cooking before the plant becomes too spiny in late season; older growth is unpleasantly prickly.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; spiny chicory is a Cretan and eastern Mediterranean coastal plant — native to the rocky, wind-exposed, salt-spray-drenched sea cliffs of Crete and other Greek islands — that is one of the most important traditional wild food plants of Cretan cuisine. The young shoots and basal leaves are collected in late winter and spring, before the plant develops its characteristic spiny character, and prepared as horta — the classic Greek-Cretan tradition of boiling or sautéing wild greens in olive oil with lemon. The flavor is pleasantly bitter in the chicory family tradition. In the garden it forms a low, spiny, somewhat cushion-like perennial with small, pale-blue chicory flowers in summer. Extremely drought-tolerant, salt-tolerant, and wind-resistant — qualities that make it outstanding for coastal and exposed Mediterranean-climate gardens where the combination of salt spray, wind, and lean, rocky soil defeats most ornamentals. An authentic plant for a Greek or Mediterranean-themed herb garden.
